summary: Security platform for discovering, red-teaming, and defending AI agents.
editor_note: Agent security is becoming a runtime problem, not a policy memo. Straiker splits the work into 3 products: Discover AI for agent and MCP visibility, Ascend AI for adversarial testing, and Defend AI for prompt-injection and data-leakage blocking. Unlike classic appsec scanners, it treats agents as active identities that can reason, call tools, and leak data.

product_evidence.claim: The homepage presents Discover AI as a way to map AI agents, MCP servers, and agentic workflows with posture monitoring and governance.
Ascend AI is described as adversarial testing for prompt injection, goal hijacking, tool misuse, and inter-agent manipulation before production.
Defend AI is positioned as runtime security that detects and blocks prompt injection, data leakage, identity abuse, memory poisoning, and tool manipulation.

market_context.primary_user: Security teams and platform teams deploying coding, productivity, or custom AI agents inside enterprises.
market_context.differentiation: Straiker covers discovery, adversarial testing, and runtime defense across agents and MCP tools instead of focusing on one security test.
market_context.why_now: The Series A lands as enterprises move agents from demos into workflows that can touch code, data, and business systems.

risks.claim: The falsifiable risk is integration depth: Straiker must keep pace with changing agent frameworks, MCP servers, and enterprise tool permissions to stay ahead of built-in controls.
risks.basis: VentureDex editorial assessment based on official product pages and the Series A announcement.
